g.data-narration: Welcome to Exception Handling Fundamentals in C++. Learn to handle runtime errors gracefully.
Traditional error handling using return codes leads to complex code. Exception handling provides a cleaner separation.
g.text: Error prone, clutters code, easy to ignore

g.data-narration: Three keywords control exception handling: try monitors code, throw signals errors, catch handles them.
The try block wraps risky code. Catch blocks follow, each handling specific exception types in sequence.
The throw keyword signals errors. You can throw any type, but class objects provide rich error information.
Catch blocks are examined sequentially. First matching handler executes. Catch by reference to avoid object slicing.
C++ provides a standard exception hierarchy. std::exception is the root, with runtime_error and logic_error as key branches.
Stack unwinding destroys local objects as exceptions propagate. This ensures RAII principles work - destructors get called.
g.text: 2. Search for catch block up the stack

g.data-narration: Use throw; (no operand) to rethrow caught exceptions to outer handlers. Allows partial cleanup before propagation.
Multiple catch blocks handle different exception types. catch(...) catches any unmatched exceptions as fallback.
The noexcept specifier promises a function won't throw. If it does, std::terminate is called immediately.
Example: A custom DivisionException class inherits from runtime_error. Check denominator before dividing.
Example: Array class throws out_of_range for invalid indices and invalid_argument for bad sizes.
g.text: Example 2: Array with Multiple Exception Types
✅ Specific handlers first, general last - Order matters!

g.data-narration: Example: Nested try-catch demonstrates rethrowing. Inner catch logs then rethrows to outer handler.
Exam tips: Remember try-throw-catch keywords, order catch blocks by specificity, and catch by reference.
Summary: Exception handling separates error detection from handling. Use try-catch-throw, custom exceptions, and remember RAII.
g.text: • Exception handling provides structured error management
• Stack unwinding ensures RAII principles work
